Name: adaptive_tensor_optimization
Title: Adaptive Tensor Optimization for Enhanced Inference Throughput on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adjustment of tensor memory layouts and access patterns based on runtime profiling can yield significant improvements in inference throughput, particularly for transformer architectures on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: While existing works like Astra and AutoKernel focus on kernel optimization through autonomous systems or multi-agent frameworks, they generally do not emphasize adaptive, runtime changes to tensor layouts based on profiling data. This proposal seeks to fill this gap by applying a profiling-driven approach to tensor memory management, which has not been comprehensively explored in the current literature.
Abstract: In the ever-evolving landscape of machine learning, the efficiency of inference processes on commodity GPUs remains a critical area for optimization. This proposal introduces Adaptive Tensor Optimization (ATO), an innovative approach that dynamically adjusts tensor memory layouts and access patterns based on real-time profiling data. By leveraging kernel-level performance metrics—such as memory bandwidth utilization, cache hit rates, and tensor-core occupancy—ATO aims to identify optimal configurations for tensor storage and access that adapt to the specific workload characteristics of transformer architectures. Preliminary experiments will involve profiling existing models under various configurations, followed by implementing a runtime optimization mechanism that adjusts tensor layouts on-the-fly. We will assess the impact of these adjustments on latency and throughput, establishing a clear link between adaptive memory management and performance gains. This work seeks to provide a reproducible framework that bridges model architecture and hardware efficiency, ultimately resulting in faster, more efficient inference on commodity GPUs.
Experiments: 1. Baseline Performance: Measure the baseline latency and throughput of standard transformer models on commodity GPUs under fixed tensor layouts.
2. Profiling Phase: Collect profiling data on memory access patterns, bandwidth utilization, and occupancy metrics during inference.
3. Adaptive Optimization: Implement ATO to adjust tensor layouts dynamically based on profiling data and re-measure throughput and latency.

4. Comparison: Conduct A/B testing to compare the performance of the adaptive approach against the baseline with fixed layouts, focusing on p50/p99 latency and throughput metrics under varying batch sizes.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The primary risk involves the potential overhead introduced by runtime profiling and tensor layout adjustments, which may negate the expected performance gains. Additionally, the effectiveness of ATO may vary depending on model architecture and workload characteristics, requiring extensive tuning across different models.
